title: MLOps Specialization (Duke/Google Cloud on Coursera)
description: 4-course specialization covering production ML systems, data pipelines, deployment patterns, and monitoring. Includes hands-on labs with Kubernetes, Kubeflow, and TFX.

title: MLOps: Machine Learning Operations (Udacity)
description: Hands-on nanodegree covering ML pipeline orchestration, model versioning, CI/CD for ML, monitoring, and cloud deployment with AWS and GCP.

title: Full Stack ML Engineer Bootcamp (4th Brain)
description: Intensive bootcamp covering ML model deployment, Docker, Kubernetes, CI/CD, model monitoring, A/B testing, and production ML system design.

title: Made With ML: MLOps Course
description: Free comprehensive course covering ML pipelines, model serving, monitoring, CI/CD, testing, and deployment patterns. Practical examples with Ray Serve and FastAPI.

title: Deploying Machine Learning Models (DeepLearning.AI)
description: Course covering model deployment patterns: batch, real-time, edge. Includes containerization with Docker, orchestration with Kubernetes, and model serving with Triton and TF Serving.

title: Practical MLOps (O'Reilly)
description: Book and course covering operationalizing ML models with Kubernetes, Docker, CI/CD pipelines, feature stores, and model monitoring. Real-world examples from production systems.

title: ZenML MLOps Fundamentals
description: Free course by ZenML covering MLOps fundamentals: pipeline orchestration, experiment tracking, model versioning, deployment, and monitoring using ZenML and various stack components.

title: CI/CD Pipeline for ML Models
description: Step-by-step workflow for setting up continuous integration and deployment for ML models.
workflow: Set up model registry: MLflow or Weights & Biases for versioning
Create training pipeline: data ingestion, preprocessing, training, evaluation
Write model validation tests: accuracy, latency, fairness, drift detection
Set up GitHub Actions workflow: trigger on model config changes
Build Docker image with model + dependencies
Run integration tests: API endpoint returns correct format and latency
Deploy to staging: Kubernetes canary deployment with 10% traffic
Run A/B test: compare new model vs baseline on real traffic
Promote to production if metrics meet SLOs
Rollback plan: keep previous model version warm for instant rollback

title: Docker + Kubernetes ML Deployment
description: Step-by-step workflow for deploying ML models with Docker and Kubernetes.
workflow: Create model serving API with FastAPI or Flask
Write Dockerfile: FROM python:3.14-slim, install deps, copy model and API
Build and test locally: docker build -t my-model . && docker run -p 8000:8000 my-model
Push to container registry: docker push registry/my-model:v1
Create Kubernetes Deployment: replicas, resource limits, GPU requests
Create Kubernetes Service: ClusterIP for internal, LoadBalancer for external
Add Horizontal Pod Autoscaler: scale based on CPU/memory/custom metrics
Configure liveness and readiness probes for model health checks
Set up rolling update strategy: maxSurge=1, maxUnavailable=0
Monitor with Prometheus: model latency, throughput, error rate metrics

title: Model Monitoring and Alerting Setup
description: Set up production model monitoring with drift detection and alerting.
workflow: Deploy MLflow or W&B for experiment tracking and model registry
Implement prediction logging: log inputs, outputs, timestamps, model version
Set up feature store: Feast or Tecton for feature serving and monitoring
Configure data drift detection: compare input distributions with KS test or PSI
Configure concept drift detection: monitor prediction accuracy over time
Set up Prometheus metrics: request latency P50/P95/P99, error rate, throughput
Create Grafana dashboards: model performance, feature distributions, drift scores
Configure alerting rules: alert on drift score > threshold or accuracy drop > 5%
Implement shadow deployment: run new model in parallel without serving traffic
Set up retraining trigger: auto-retrain when drift exceeds threshold

title: Feature Store Setup with Feast
description: Step-by-step workflow for setting up a feature store with Feast for ML feature management.
workflow: Install Feast: pip install feast
Create feature repository: feast init my_feature_repo
Define entity and feature views in feature_definitions.py
Set up offline store: BigQuery, Snowflake, or Redshift for training data
Set up online store: Redis or DynamoDB for low-latency serving
Register feature definitions: feast apply
Materialize features: feast materialize to populate online store
Build training dataset: feast.get_historical_features for model training
Serve features in production: feast.get_online_features for real-time inference
Monitor feature drift: compare online vs offline feature statistics

title: ML Model Testing Pipeline
description: Comprehensive testing pipeline for ML models before production deployment.
workflow: Unit tests: test data preprocessing functions with known inputs/outputs
Integration tests: test full pipeline end-to-end with sample data
Model performance tests: verify accuracy > threshold on held-out test set
Latency tests: measure P50, P95, P99 inference latency with production load
Memory tests: verify model fits in GPU/CPU memory with production batch size
Fairness tests: check model performance across demographic groups
Robustness tests: test with adversarial inputs, edge cases, missing data
Data drift tests: compare input feature distributions with training data
Schema validation: verify input schema matches expected format
Smoke test: deploy to staging and verify health endpoint responds

title: MLflow Experiment Tracking and Model Registry
description: Python code for tracking ML experiments and registering models with MLflow.
code:
import mlflow
import mlflow.sklearn
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier

# Set tracking URI and experiment
mlflow.set_tracking_uri("http://127.0.0.1:5000")
mlflow.set_experiment("fraud-detection")

# Train and log model
with mlflow.start_run(run_name="rf-v1"):
    # Log parameters
    mlflow.log_param("n_estimators", 100)
    mlflow.log_param("max_depth", 10)
    
    # Train model
    model = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, max_depth=10)
    model.fit(X_train, y_train)
    
    # Log metrics
    accuracy = model.score(X_test, y_test)
    mlflow.log_metric("accuracy", accuracy)
    mlflow.log_metric("f1_score", f1_score(y_test, model.predict(X_test)))
    
    # Log model
    mlflow.sklearn.log_model(model, "model")

# Register model
mlflow.register_model(
    f"runs:/{mlflow.active_run().info.run_id}/model",
    "fraud-detection-model"
)

title: FastAPI Model Serving with Health Checks
description: Production FastAPI server for ML model serving with health checks and metrics.
code:
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException
from pydantic import BaseModel
import time

app = FastAPI(title="ML Model Server", version="1.0.0")
model = None
model_version = "v1.0.0"
request_count = 0

class PredictionRequest(BaseModel):
    features: list[float]

class PredictionResponse(BaseModel):
    prediction: float
    probability: float
    model_version: str
    latency_ms: float

@app.on_event("startup")
async def load_model():
    global model
    # Load model here
    model = "loaded"

@app.get("/health")
async def health():
    return {"status": "healthy", "model_version": model_version}

@app.post("/predict", response_model=PredictionResponse)
async def predict(request: PredictionRequest):
    global request_count
    start = time.time()
    result = model.predict([request.features])
    latency = (time.time() - start) * 1000
    request_count += 1
    return PredictionResponse(
        prediction=float(result[0]),
        probability=float(result[1]),
        model_version=model_version,
        latency_ms=latency
    )

title: Docker Compose for ML Stack
description: Docker Compose configuration for a complete ML serving stack with model server, feature store, monitoring, and model registry.
code:
services:
  model-server:
    build: ./model-server
    ports: ["127.0.0.1:8000:8000"]
    environment:
      MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI: http://mlflow:5000
      FEAST_ONLINE_URL: redis://redis:6379
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "curl", "-sf", "http://127.0.0.1:8000/health"]
      interval: 30s
    deploy:
      resources:
        reservations:
          devices:
            - driver: nvidia
              count: all
              capabilities: [gpu]

  mlflow:
    image: ghcr.io/mlflow/mlflow:v2.18.0
    ports: ["127.0.0.1:5000:5000"]
    environment:
      MLFLOW_BACKEND_STORE_URI: postgresql://mlflow:mlflow@db:5432/mlflow

  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    ports: ["127.0.0.1:6379:6379"]

  prometheus:
    image: prom/prometheus
    ports: ["127.0.0.1:9090:9090"]

  grafana:
    image: grafana/grafana
    ports: ["127.0.0.1:3000:3000"]

title: GitHub Actions CI/CD for ML Models
description: GitHub Actions workflow for automated ML model training, testing, and deployment.
code:
name: ML Pipeline
on:
  push:
    paths: ["model/**", "data/**"]

jobs:
  train-and-deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: {python-version: "3.14"}
      - run: pip install -r requirements.txt
      - run: python train.py
      - run: pytest tests/ -v
      - run: python validate_model.py --threshold 0.95
      - name: Deploy to staging
        if: success()
        run: |
          docker build -t model-server:${{ github.sha }} .
          docker push registry/model-server:${{ github.sha }}
          kubectl set image deployment/model-server \
            model-server=registry/model-server:${{ github.sha }} \
            --namespace=staging

title: Data Drift Detection with Evidently
description: Python code for detecting data and concept drift in production ML models using Evidently.
code:
from evidently import ColumnMapping
from evidently.report import Report
from evidently.metric_preset import DataDriftPreset, RegressionPreset
import pandas as pd

# Load reference (training) and current (production) data
reference_data = pd.read_parquet("data/train.parquet")
current_data = pd.read_parquet("data/production_2026_05.parquet")

column_mapping = ColumnMapping(
    target="target",
    prediction="prediction",
    numerical_features=["age", "income", "score"],
    categorical_features=["category", "region"]
)

# Generate drift report
drift_report = Report(metrics=[
    DataDriftPreset(),
    RegressionPreset()
])
drift_report.run(
    reference_data=reference_data,
    current_data=current_data,
    column_mapping=column_mapping
)

# Check drift status
drift_result = drift_report.as_dict()
if drift_result["data_drift"]["share"] > 0.3:
    print("WARNING: Data drift detected! Retraining recommended.")
    # Trigger retraining pipeline
else:
    print("No significant drift detected. Model is healthy.")

title: MLflow
description: Open-source platform for ML lifecycle management: experiment tracking, model registry, model serving, and deployment. Supports PyTorch, TensorFlow, HuggingFace, and custom models.

title: Kubeflow
description: Kubernetes-native ML platform for managing ML workflows: pipelines, training jobs, hyperparameter tuning, model serving, and notebook servers. Best for teams already on Kubernetes.

title: Weights & Biases (W&B)
description: Experiment tracking, dataset versioning, model evaluation, and hyperparameter sweeps platform. Collaborative dashboards for ML teams with rich visualization.

title: Evidently
description: Open-source ML monitoring tool for data drift detection, model quality monitoring, and data quality checks. Generates interactive HTML reports and integrates with MLflow.

title: Feast
description: Open-source feature store for ML: online serving (Redis/DynamoDB) for real-time inference, offline computing (BigQuery/Snowflake) for training. Prevents feature skew between training and serving.

title: BentoML
description: Open-source framework for building production-ready ML services. Handles model packaging, API serving, containerization, and deployment to Kubernetes, AWS, GCP, and Azure.

title: DVC (Data Version Control)
description: Git-like version control for data and ML models. Tracks data files, models, and metrics alongside code. Enables reproducible experiments and data pipelines.

title: Great Expectations
description: Open-source data quality platform: define expectations (assertions) about your data, validate data at pipeline boundaries, and generate data quality reports.

title: Shadow Deployment Pattern
description: Deploy a new model version alongside the production model. Route real traffic to the production model but also send copies to the shadow model. Compare outputs without affecting users. Promote when shadow model meets SLOs.

title: Feature Store Pattern
description: Centralize feature computation and serving. Train on offline features (batch), serve from online store (low-latency). Prevents training-serving skew by using the same feature definitions for both. Feast is the canonical open-source implementation.

title: Canary Deployment Pattern
description: Route a small percentage (1-5%) of production traffic to a new model version. Monitor metrics (latency, accuracy, error rate). Gradually increase traffic if metrics are good. Rollback instantly if issues detected.

title: Model Registry Pattern
description: Centralize model versioning, metadata, and lifecycle management. Track model versions, stages (staging/production/archived), and lineage. MLflow Model Registry is the standard open-source implementation.

title: Data Pipeline Pattern (Medallion Architecture)
description: Organize data in three layers: Bronze (raw ingestion), Silver (cleaned, deduplicated), Gold (feature-engineered, ready for ML). Enables incremental processing and clear data lineage.

title: MLflow Quickstart Guide
description: Official quickstart for MLflow: install, track experiments, register models, and serve models with MLflow.

title: Kubeflow Pipelines Tutorial
description: Official Kubeflow Pipelines tutorial covering pipeline creation, component definition, execution, and monitoring on Kubernetes.

title: DVC Getting Started Guide
description: Official DVC tutorial covering data versioning, pipeline definitions, experiment tracking, and collaboration with Git and DVC remote storage.

title: Designing Machine Learning Systems (Chip Huyen)
description: Comprehensive guide to ML system design: data management, feature engineering, model evaluation, deployment patterns, monitoring, and continuous learning. The canonical MLOps reference.

title: Building Machine Learning Powered Applications (Emmanuel Ameisen)
description: Practical guide to going from ML prototype to production: iterative development, data pipelines, model iteration, deployment, and monitoring. Focuses on the engineering challenges of ML products.

title: Machine Learning Engineering in Action (Ben Wilson)
description: Hands-on guide to ML engineering patterns: feature stores, CI/CD for ML, model testing, deployment strategies, and monitoring. Written by MLflow maintainers.
